questions.prompt: Your BuggyShop checkout suite has 300 tests and they all pass. What can you correctly conclude?
options: Checkout is now free of defects
The 300 cases you checked found no defects; others may still exist
Checkout has been exhaustively tested
No further testing of checkout is ever needed
questions.explanation: Testing shows the presence of defects, not their absence. Green tests only mean the cases you ran did not surface a bug, not that none remain.

questions.prompt: BuggyShop's coupon field accepts any text string of any length. A teammate proposes testing every possible value to be safe. Which principle explains why this plan fails?
options: Defects cluster
Testing is context-dependent
Exhaustive testing is impossible
Early testing saves money
questions.explanation: The input space is effectively infinite, so exhaustive testing is impossible. You sample with techniques like equivalence partitioning instead.

questions.prompt: A spec says tax should apply before shipping, but the code applies it after. Catching this in a requirements review versus after release best demonstrates which principle?
options: Early testing (shift-left) saves time and money
The pesticide paradox
The absence-of-errors fallacy
Exhaustive testing is impossible
questions.explanation: Finding the defect early, during review, costs minutes; finding it after release costs refunds and rework. That is the shift-left principle.

questions.prompt: Your bug log shows 70% of BuggyShop defects live in signup and checkout, while the static pages have almost none. What should this drive you to do?
options: Spread test effort evenly across every page for fairness
Concentrate more testing on signup and checkout
Stop testing signup and checkout since bugs are already known
Test only the static pages that currently look clean
questions.explanation: Defects cluster, so the highest-risk modules deserve the most attention. Signup and checkout are where the payoff is.

questions.prompt: BuggyShop v1.1 adds coupon stacking. You rerun the unchanged v1.0 suite, everything passes, but a stacking bug ships. What went wrong?
options: The tests were poorly written from the start
The reused tests went stale and never exercised the new path (pesticide paradox)
Exhaustive testing was attempted and failed
The defect-free product still failed its users
questions.explanation: The pesticide paradox: repeating the same tests stops finding new bugs. The suite must be updated with cases for the changed feature.

questions.prompt: Why would you test BuggyShop's payment flow far more rigorously than its footer links?
options: Because exhaustive testing is impossible
Because testing is context-dependent and payment carries higher risk
Because defects never appear in footer links
Because early testing only applies to payments
questions.explanation: Testing is context-dependent: the level and type of testing scale with risk and context. Money-handling flows justify deeper testing than a footer.

questions.prompt: BuggyShop checkout passes every test, yet it charges international users in the wrong currency and they abandon. Which principle does this illustrate?
options: The pesticide paradox
Defects cluster
The absence-of-errors fallacy
Exhaustive testing is impossible
questions.explanation: Absence-of-errors fallacy: a product can be defect-free against its tests yet still fail because it does not meet real user needs.
